A campus garden recorded as it changes — scanned, overlaid in mixed reality, and read against itself across time.

Comparison · 01

Physical, then augmented.

Drag the divider across the same frame: on one side the garden as it stands, on the other the registered point cloud projected back over it. The seam is coloured by the M3C2 distance ramp used throughout.

About this view — the overlay is a generated stand-in scattered over your own photograph. To use a real capture, swap the canvas for a second photo (the registered/AR render) or a <video> — the clip-path divider works the same on any layer.

Sensors · 03

What the garden registered.

Environmental readings logged across five points on the site over a winter week. Switch metric, hover to read values, tap the legend to isolate a sensor.

Sensor data comparison
—

Loading real data — the series here are generated stand-ins. Replace the genSeries() output with your logged values (one array of {t, <sensor>:value} rows per metric, or parse a CSV) — the axes, tabs, legend and tooltip adapt to whatever ranges you pass in.

About · 04

On recording change.

This project treats a single campus garden as a specimen observed across time. The same ground is captured as a point cloud, registered to fixed markers, and projected back over the site in mixed reality so that the record can be walked rather than only viewed.

Where conventional architectural drawing fixes a building at the instant of completion, a garden refuses that stillness. Foliage advances, a sapling thickens, a path is re-laid, a bench is rebuilt. The aim is not to arrest those changes but to make them legible — to let natural and human processes of growth and recession enter the design language rather than be cleaned away as noise.

Method

Two epochs are aligned through marker-anchored registration and differenced with the M3C2 algorithm, which measures signed distance along local surface normals. Growth toward the sensor reads warm; recession reads cool; stable surfaces fall near zero. The same ramp colours every quantitative element here, so the eye learns one scale and reuses it.

Reading the record

The index holds the physical garden against its augmented overlay; the plates collect the on-site sessions; the 3D view lets you move freely through the differenced cloud. Together they describe a garden less as an object than as a relationship between two times.
